rationale: The citing paper uses Nelson and Meyvis (2008) as direct evidence for a satiation/hedonic adaptation account in which utility declines during ongoing consumption and interruptions can intensify experience. This citation is not just in a broad list: it appears in the core theoretical framing, in the goal-progress theory table, and in interpretation of why breaks might affect engagement. That prior finding helps define one of the candidate utility-shape mechanisms the paper compares against alternatives such as the goal-gradient account.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Nelson and Meyvis (2008) as a conceptual foundation for its satiation-based explanation of goal progress, using it to motivate the idea that adaptation over continuous activity and interruptions can shape motivation dynamics in online learning.
